data.title: BlackRock's Global Chief Fixed Income Investment Officer Shares Investment Principles
data.description:
Source: Hard Lessons podcast; Compiled by: Felix, PANews

Rick Rieder, Chief Investment Officer of Global Fixed Income at BlackRock, recently appeared on Morgan Stanley's Hard Lessons podcast to share his experience on investing, liquidity, and discipline. At BlackRock, Rick Rieder helps manage up to $2.7 trillion in assets across global bond and multi-asset markets and serves as Chairman of BlackRock's Wide Investment Council.

In the conversation, Rick Rieder explained the drivers of long-term returns and why seeking perspectives different from market consensus is crucial. PANews has compiled the highlights of the conversation.

Host: You've worked at BlackRock for 17 years, which is a long time. How has your journey at BlackRock been?

Rieder: I remember back in 2009, when I was running a hedge fund, all my partners were discussing whether we should join BlackRock. I remember saying that this could become the center of finance, but I never expected the company to grow to the size it is today. We joined just before the acquisitions of BGI and iShares, which significantly altered the landscape of the ETF industry. Think about it, now we manage over $14 trillion in assets, and when I joined, we were just a tiny fraction of that. It has become an amazing place, so it's been a very interesting experience. Host: You just used the word "center," and I think that's very apt, because when you were managing only a few billion dollars, did you ever imagine that one day you would be talking about managing trillions of dollars? Rieder: I'm the chairman of the board of a charter school in Newark, New Jersey. Once, I gave a presentation about the size of the funds we manage. I asked, "Does anyone know how many zeros are after a trillion?" Someone answered, but I didn't actually know myself. I had to stop and actually count the zeros in my head. It's kind of funny, but when you think about that massive scale, it's truly incredible. Of course, in the portfolios I manage, we have an almost insane level of precision in every detail. Therefore, managing at this scale doesn't feel out of control; it feels very manageable. Host: Do you think pure scale advantage is one of the secrets to your success? What exactly is the secret to success? Rieder: Scale does bring some benefits, such as being able to see huge flows of funds across different asset classes and thousands of different scenarios. Statistically speaking, being able to see so many different things naturally increases your chances of winning. By the way, it's not that all my decisions were right, or that many were right; the point is that you have the ability to observe and judge, "Okay, that makes sense," and to see relative value in places you wouldn't even think of. It's like being able to put all the pieces together. It gives you the ability to actually try and effectively put the puzzle together.

Host: So I'd like to shift the topic and try to draw some wisdom from you, and analyze a little bit about the process you went through when reflecting on your career. At times, your views contradicted the consensus, but you turned out to be right.

Rieder: Perhaps I need to clarify what you mean by "wisdom." I really don't think I should be described as having wisdom, because I feel it all depends on having to do a lot of work. At least for me, there are no unique skills that are innate. I just try to put all the pieces together and then hold firm to that belief.

Some people will confidently double down if others don't believe them, but I'm not so good at that. However, I think I'm better at observing things like technology. I'm a super tech geek. Whenever a new technology is released, I have to be first in line. My wife always laughs at me for having to run to the store, have to queue, have to be with everyone, and have to understand what it is. I just love technology. I'll never forget when electric cars first came out. Back then, many people said, "This won't work. Batteries are too expensive. You can't compete. There are so many established giants in the traditional gasoline car market. This is absolutely impossible." I remember doing all the research and finding that it wasn't really a car business, but an energy business. You have to consider whether it's efficient? Can you create real economies of scale around it? I remember sitting alone in a conference room, like the only fool. By the way, I don't mind expressing my strong convictions many times, but I'm telling you, getting involved early in these things is crucial. I remember my first test drive, and my feeling was, "Wow, it's fast, clean, and quiet. This is a better product." I have this feeling about many new things, like when the initial Mac computers and AirPods were released, I thought, "This works great, it's very different." But for me, the next step is to understand: How big is the total potential market (TAM)? What is the market size? How do you reduce costs over time? What will the back-end cash flow look like?

Host: You just described a very disciplined and humble process. But sometimes going it alone is also a lonely process. When everyone else says you might be crazy. What do you do in those moments?

Rieder: I would say that 99% of the time, it's thanks to teamwork, because everyone tends to conform to the consensus, and I'm no exception. When there are people who hold non-consensus views and are different from you, you often double-check with them, asking yourself, "What are we missing? Where did we go wrong?" Then I like to read a lot of analyses from people with different viewpoints to see why they think it makes no sense.

So I tend to talk to people who share my views, but for those who disagree, I try to research them and build my confidence. But as I've said before, over time I've learned that we're not in a business that "must be right," we're in a business that creates returns for our clients. The reality is that market perceptions can be wrong for much longer periods. I remember learning about the "efficient market hypothesis" in school, and I actually think they should throw that theory away because it's completely detached from reality. I believe the market often makes mistakes, but you have to survive because by the time the market theoretically becomes right, your capital might already be depleted. So I try to stay in the market. When people disagree with me, I'm not good at doubling down because that would damage my confidence, but I try to stick to my existing positions and try to analyze my thinking more deeply. It's like telling myself: this will definitely work. Host: I think at least Keynes said, "The market may remain irrational for longer than you can remain liquid." As a sell-side analyst, asking this might seem a bit self-serving, but do you find it more helpful to read articles by people who broadly agree with you to confirm your ideas, or to seek out those who disagree with you, stress-test them, and find where you might be going wrong? Rieder: The most mature answer would be: challenge yourself with people who hold different views, but I haven't done that enough. There are people I respect and trust, and I enjoy reading their analysis regardless of whether they're right or wrong. But when things are very contrary to consensus, I try to read a lot of the ideas of those who agree. Everyone tends to act in unison. I think social media has exacerbated this phenomenon. Everyone is going in the same direction, and as you get older and have been in this business longer, you'll find that there's a moment when everyone is acting, and prices move accordingly, and then you realize, "Okay, now that's the move, it's time to go against the trend." I think the market has largely become a casino mechanism now; you see everyone queuing up on one side, and at that point, as a contrarian investor going against the consensus, I think the profits become more substantial. Especially when news flow is very dense, this actually becomes a pretty good trading strategy: if the market leans to one side, you short it; if the market leans to the other side, you continue to trade against it. You'll realize you're on the trend's trajectory.

Host: But I also have to say, if the entire market is already on one side, then making money this way is even harder, because the price has already reflected everything. So what do you plan to do? Maybe sometimes you've carefully considered all the details, but ultimately the trade still didn't succeed; maybe the consensus was right, and there was a reason for it. When you recall those unsuccessful experiences, what comes to mind first?

Rieder: The trades I made that went wrong immediately come to mind, because I think you learn a lot more from making mistakes. People tend to forget, or hope that the results of a lifetime of training result in more correct than wrong answers. But I remember about 20 years ago, shortly after I graduated, in a bond coupon transaction, I thought I was definitely right, so I wanted to buy more.

That was an incredibly painful lesson: the whole world thinks you're wrong, you have to exit, or you have to reduce your position size so that one security doesn't ruin your entire career. That was my first lesson: you have to manage your risk, you have to manage your position size. It's a small industry, and everyone can sense you're in trouble. It was a very bad situation, and it's a pretty brutal industry, and when people think you're on the wrong side, they put pressure on you. I'll always remember Peloton (the fitness equipment company). I was one of its earliest investors, and it was incredible how I watched their technology develop, then the pandemic hit, which was lucky for them, and then the stock price exploded. Then you become even more immersed in its ecosystem. This is also one of those situations that makes you feel really bad: you think you're right about it, but suddenly it starts to go against you, and a lot of people are talking about why it's falling. I learned a few things in that process: how do you manage cash flow? The company grew too fast, and people say it's because of the COVID-19 pandemic, maybe that's partly true, but "stop-loss" is a very good habit. And I have a theory: if you're right three-quarters of the time, and then you go wrong, well, it's time to cut your losses and move on. Because in this industry, just one mistake can hurt you in an extremely drastic way. So, I firmly believe this, especially in bonds: diversify, make sure you have liquid assets. A 65% or 60% win rate is fine. A 70% win rate on illiquid assets is good too. I really think that as long as you're right more often than you're wrong, it's okay. Like running a casino. But in the stock market, the reason I remember those major mistakes so vividly is because in the stock market, you experience explosive profits and crashes, and that's exactly what you have to think about. You know, you have to cultivate the investments that make you money while managing the ones that make you lose. Make sure you keep your positions at a level where you can afford to lose. Host: Regarding the Peloton deal, do you think you were wrong in the first place, or was it simply a matter of timing? Rieder: Frankly, I think the company could have changed course, changed direction, but they didn't. I think they were too slow to change course. As someone with a credit background who later moved into equity investing, the most important thing I learned is this: in credit, you focus on cash flow, interest coverage ratio, collateral, and all those other metrics; with companies, you think about their business model and how they generate cash flow. But one thing I learned is that the people managing the company are extremely important, not just the individuals, but the team that runs the company. Because companies always evolve, industries evolve, technology evolves, and you have to know how to transform. Most of the most successful companies started out completely different from what they are now. Then they transformed, entered a field full of opportunities, and what they did initially might have been a good idea at the time, but later had to change. Now, I spend more time talking to CEOs to gauge whether they understand the data? Do they understand the business? Is someone a good operator? For me, that's the whole point.

Host: What allows you to sift through these details and make such a distinction?

Rieder: In the tech world, some people have a keen insight, able to think about where the world is going and then adapt their businesses accordingly. But there are also people, as you said, haphazard and disorganized, trying to do too much or chasing trends, and ending up lagging behind. Over time, I've found some very unique individuals who are exceptionally good at positioning their companies well before the wave hits. Some are on the crest of the wave, able to talk big, but the key is the person who can truly understand what's happening in the chaos and know how to adapt.

Host: I think this skill is actually universal in many different fields.

Rieder: That's true. But let me tell you one thing I've done very poorly throughout my career: reading.

There's so much analysis and research, so much brilliant stuff to read. If we were to divide the work and say, "You read this, you read that," I feel like I have to read as much as possible myself. I have meetings every month, and I think we have a great team. We brainstorm, and then I have to lock myself in a room and figure everything out myself. Hopefully, AI will be able to do this work for me in the future. But I guess I'll still use AI to help me organize my thoughts, just that it will allow me to absorb more information much faster. Host: Yes, my firm prediction is that AI will present all the information to you, but you'll still have to go through it yourself. Rieder: I think that's true. The decision-making process should become more efficient because I've found that as humans, you can only think on a limited number of levels. When I look at our portfolio and think about risk, if you can think multidimensionally and use technology for stress testing, scenario analysis, and combining different parts, I think that will be very valuable. Host: When you look back on your entire career, what lessons have been the most painful? Rieder: For me, the financial crisis is definitely one of the first, second, and third most painful lessons. Because I started a hedge fund before the financial crisis. You imagine financial assets, when all assets are falling highly correlated, and we were using some leverage, suddenly everything was falling in sync. Host: You started your hedge fund during the financial crisis? Rieder: Or rather, a few months before the crisis, when I actually thought it was a good time because things were becoming volatile, interesting, etc., but I didn't anticipate the series of events that followed. We started out doing well, everything was going smoothly, and then suddenly, the whole world was disrupted in a way that I found incredibly unexpected (including policy direction, etc.). I will never forget the pressure of that period. Some days, I remember walking into the office, down that long hallway, and muttering to myself, “This is going to be tough, this is going to be tough.” I tried to cheer myself up until I reached the door, but the truth was, it was still tough. I’ll never forget that period. But like you said, it taught me a lot and changed the way I think. To this day, I still think about liquidity, leverage, and tail risk. People always think they’ll never encounter that kind of extreme tail risk. But I think, what if that extremely unexpected situation happens and knocks me out? Of course, I’ve had to fight that fear throughout my career, telling myself, “Okay, that probably won’t happen tomorrow. We have to be in the investment business, we have to generate returns, we have to take risks.” So you have to think: Where is my escape pod? How do I think about my exit strategy for almost every asset, every position, every portfolio structure? If you know what your exit strategy is, if you know where your escape pod is, it helps you plan, and if something happens, you can execute Plan B. That being said, we’re in a risk business. I have to say, I like pressure, though not too much, but I think anyone in the investment industry must enjoy it. Host: Regarding crises, perhaps the extreme version of risk, it lets you see exactly how bad things can get. This is often attributed to Churchill's quote: "The plans of a war are useless, but the process of making those plans is essential." I feel that's your daily reality. Rieder: Absolutely. You'll find that in the investment industry, a large part of the factors driving valuations are sentiment. Markets often fall five times faster than they rise. People make money slowly, but lose it extremely quickly. Look at the recent performance of certain commodities; making money is slow, then suddenly "bang," it's all gone. You'll experience situations where a news item or event suddenly appears, and your investment logic and structural position layout are completely disrupted. Therefore, investing is not just about judging whether an asset is worthwhile, but equally important is predicting how people will interpret that asset two or three months from now. This incorporates a bit of psychology, something I learned very poorly in school, but I've picked up some of it later in my investment practice.

data.title: This AI "lobster" can trade for you—dare you give it a try?
data.description:
I came for the lobster, but stayed because of the covered call options.

If putting crustaceans and options trading together confuses you, let me clarify: I'm talking about OpenClaw, the wildly popular AI assistant that some adventurous people are currently using to manage their investments. And yes, what initially attracted me was OpenClaw's lobster-themed design.

Unlike chatbots like ChatGPT (which are practically ancient history), OpenClaw can proactively perform tasks for you, such as booking and buying show tickets. It can also provide investment strategy advice and even trade securities for you while you're lounging on the beach.

What could possibly go wrong?

What I want to say isn't that OpenClaw is some kind of marvel, but rather that AI is coming—to be precise, it's already here, and it's being used more and more by Wall Street institutions and individuals. I'm not suggesting you jump on the bandwagon, but understanding what's happening might be more beneficial. OpenClaw was created last November by Peter Steinberger (now working at OpenAI), an Austrian "ambient coding" programmer. This ecosystem, featuring lobster-themed naming conventions, reportedly boasts 38 million monthly active users, many of whom are in China. The OpenClaw channel "Friends of the Crustacean" on Discord is a popular entry point, where people frequently visit the AI. The channel is active and often highly technical (my nickname there is Andre the Seal). To better understand this, I spoke with Thomas Peterffy, Chairman of Interactive Brokers Group, who has long been at the forefront of fintech. The 81-year-old Peterffy also happens to be one of Wall Street's wealthiest people, largely due to Interactive Brokers' stock price rising from $16 to $72 since April 2022. "I was a computer programmer when I started my business, so I've always been building algorithms," Peterffy said. "For years we've been providing our clients with algorithms that automate order execution. AI is like a new, more advanced computer language. We're heavy users. There's no doubt it's the future." Interactive Brokers' clients use AI both through external AI agents like OpenClaw and through the brokerage's internal tools provided by partner company Reflexivity. “The problem has never been a lack of data, but rather that it would take days to process it. Now it only takes about eight minutes,” said Jan Siraghi, CEO and co-founder of Reflexivity. He holds a PhD in Economics and Finance from Harvard University and previously helped Stan Druckenmiller manage its global macro portfolio. On Wednesday morning, markets were jubilant after the US and Iran announced a two-week ceasefire. I asked Siraghi to set up a “TACO” trade for me (TACO being an abbreviation for “Trump always chickens out”). The algorithm returned an 11-page report concluding that “the first day of TACO trading was incredibly successful,” and recommended (along with entry points) a strong long position in crude oil and the CBOE Volatility Index (VIX); a moderate long position in the State Street Energy Select Sector SPDR ETF; a moderate short position in the S&P 500; and a slight short position in US Treasuries. Interactive Brokers rigorously vets clients who use external tools like OpenClaw. “We have procedures that capture their instructions and review them instantly to ensure compliance with regulatory requirements and applicable margin rules,” Peter Fee said. What if OpenClaw sells my stocks against my will? “That’s not my problem,” he said. “That’s your problem. We just check the orders and execute them.” At the emerging stock trading platform Public.com, these kinds of questions are closer to users—the platform has gone all-in (or “fully shell-in”) on AI. Public’s clients can connect OpenClaw directly to their accounts to analyze and—yes—trade securities. Public, headquartered in New York, is a privately held company backed by Accel and Tiger Global Management venture capital. Jannick Malling, Public’s Danish co-founder and co-CEO, says the platform manages billions of dollars in assets and has millions of users. He stated that Public began exploring AI in 2023, aiming to create "the world's first AI-powered brokerage firm." The launch of OpenClaw is rapidly heating up this endeavor. "Trading has evolved from phone orders to web-based trading, then to mobile apps, and now to AI-powered brokerage," Malling said. "You just type in, 'Find me three different ways to make $10,000 this month by selling covered call options on my portfolio,' which is a huge shift." Setting up OpenClaw requires some "digital work." "It took me about an hour and a half to two hours to set it up," said Dean Allens, a 19-year-old trader from Westminster, Colorado. He may be the typical client Public is looking to attract. Allens recently used OpenClaw to build and execute a put option strategy for the Invesco QQQ Trust ETF and used it to find and buy put options on Hims & Hers Health. At the institutional level, Clive Asnes, who manages the large quantitative hedge fund AQR, was once skeptical of AI. "I might have slowed us down by two or three years," he said, "but when you have something that's worked for 30 years, it's rational to do so. All these decisions involve money, time, and risk." The 59-year-old Asnes mentioned that his fund's analysts had spent decades sifting through credit card data and analyzing the positive and negative wording in financial statements. Now, he has completely changed his mind, saying, "Machine learning—a branch of AI—has proven to be better than our past methods." Asnes said that historically, AQR's holdings were conceptually built on a 50/50 basis of historical data and economic logic. Now, thanks to AI, it's more like two-thirds data and one-third logic. Has AQR's return improved? He said, "Well, it's been a good five years." But Asnes isn't so optimistic about AI for retail investors. "I'm cautious about a world where everyone's talking about machine learning," he said. "The idea that you can use it to pick just a few stocks might not be any worse than other methods, but I don't see any magic in it." As for OpenClaw, enjoy it. Just be careful not to get caught in it.

data.title: Pharos raises $44 million in funding, with 12 projects raising $62.7 million.
data.description:
Compiled by: Jinse Finance

This week, a total of 12 blockchain startups raised $62.7 million.

Largest Funding Round This Week

Pharos completed a $44 million Series A funding round, with investors including SNZ Holding, Chainlink, and Flow Traders.

Pharos is a deeply parallel Layer-1 blockchain network designed to support high-speed, scalable, and decentralized applications. It is EVM compatible, allowing Ethereum dApp developers to use familiar toolchains while enjoying Pharos features such as 1-second final confirmation, reduced storage costs, and enhanced security through the AsyncBFT consensus mechanism. Pharos is committed to driving the development of DeFi, Real-World Assets (RWA), Decentralized Physical Infrastructure (DePIN), and cross-chain interoperability, and achieves innovation through a unified account system across multiple virtual machines.

**Enhanced Raises $1 Million in Pre-seed and Seed Funding Rounds** Enhanced is a DeFi protocol focused on structured options returns. Its goal is to make complex derivative strategies more accessible through an intuitive, results-oriented product experience, while improving yields by optimizing auction mechanisms and capital efficiency. The team is expanding from mainstream crypto assets to a wider range of on-chain assets, including tokenized real-world assets. **Giggles Raises $1.2 Million in Pre-seed Funding Rounds** Giggles is a Web3 social network where users can invest early in videos and earn rewards as the content spreads. Investors include 1kx, Virtuals Protocol, Social Graph Ventures, Night Capital, and Noar Ventures. By combining social interaction with market mechanisms, it creates a completely new model for content discovery and monetization. Users are not only viewers but also stakeholders, thus aligning incentives between creators and audiences. Built on blockchain, Giggles supports ownership, token rewards, and transparent interaction, creating a crypto-native ecosystem driven by viral content, memes, and community collaboration. Other Funding CAEX Exchange completed a funding round (amount undisclosed) with investors including OKX Ventures, Hashkey Capital, VPBank Securities, and LynkiD. CAEX is a crypto asset trading platform targeting the Vietnamese market, committed to providing compliant, transparent, and secure digital asset trading. Its features include compliance with Vietnamese laws and regulations, KYC/KYB verification, trading based on the Vietnamese Dong (VND), customer asset segregation, and multi-layered security mechanisms including cold wallets and HSMs. The platform positions itself as a trusted gateway for investors seeking a locally compliant trading experience. Oh has completed a $7.5 million Series A funding round, with investors including Maven 11 Capital, L1D, Auros Global, Hashed, Maelstrom, Cadenza, Cyber ​​Fund, Kosmos Ventures, and Tangent Ventures. Oh is a Web3 AI project dedicated to developing uncensored generative AI models and applications. Its ecosystem includes OhChat (a platform for immersive interaction with AI characters "superModels") and the $OH token (used for platform governance and functionality). Oh aims to decentralize AI governance and provide monetization opportunities for creators through interoperable AI characters. Transak has completed a strategic funding round (amount undisclosed), with Gobi Partners as the investor. Transak is a Web3 payment infrastructure provider offering seamless exchange (deposits and withdrawals) between fiat and cryptocurrency. Designed for developers, it can be easily integrated into wallets, dApps, and platforms via SDK or API, and supports multiple payment methods (bank cards, bank transfers, Apple Pay, Google Pay), covering 155+ countries, and includes a KYC process. Transak also offers advanced tools such as Transak One (one-step fiat to smart contract) and Transak Stream (fast withdrawal to bank accounts), and holds compliance licenses in the US, Canada, the UK, and other countries. Libera has completed its Series A funding round (amount undisclosed), with investors including GSR Markets LTD, Monk’s Hill Ventures, and AlloyX. Libera is a blockchain-based platform focused on the tokenization of Real-World Assets (RWAs), enabling institutions to access traditionally illiquid financial products. Its goal is to connect traditional finance with Web3, providing the infrastructure for issuing, managing, and distributing tokenized funds, emphasizing compliance and transparency. Splyce Finance has completed a strategic funding round (amount undisclosed), with investors including the Sui Foundation, Stellar Development Foundation, Solana Foundation, Lucid Drakes, Sarson Funds, and Kin Capital. Splyce Finance is a Solana-based DeFi protocol focused on delivering real-world yields through fully on-chain institutional-grade assets. It supports permissionless access to tokenized RWAs and automated yield strategies while maintaining composability and 24/7 liquidity. The platform also offers decentralized ETFs (dETFs), allowing users to diversify their crypto asset portfolios through a single token. Darklake has completed an acquisition funding round (amount undisclosed), with Sol Strategies (formerly Cypherpunk Holdings) as the investor. Darklake is a Solana-based zero-knowledge infrastructure project dedicated to building privacy-first systems for on-chain applications. Its technology stack supports secure computation and collaboration for applications, wallets, and protocols while maintaining composability and Solana's high-speed settlement. Its products include a privacy-preserving ZK-AMM, a perpetual DEX under development, and the core proof technology Zyga Protocol. The project focuses on reusable proofs, cryptographic states, and developer-oriented tools. NanoVita has completed its Series A funding round (amount undisclosed), with investors including K24 Ventures, Landscape Capital, and WestLabs Venture. NanoVita is a crypto-native DeSci protocol that integrates nanotechnology, AI bio-intelligence, and tokenized health data into a unified on-chain ecosystem. It enables users to own and monetize their health data, while supporting decentralized research and personalized medicine. By combining AI inference, blockchain transparency, and the RWA dataset, NanoVita transforms research findings into verifiable on-chain value. GoSats has raised $5 million in Series A funding, with investors including Konvoy Ventures, Y Combinator, and Taisu Ventures. GoSats is a Bitcoin rewards platform where users can earn "sats" (the smallest unit of Bitcoin) by shopping at partner brands. It combines everyday spending with crypto applications, offering Bitcoin cashback instead of traditional fiat currency rewards. The platform also offers gift cards, brand partnerships, and gamified rewards to increase user engagement and long-term accumulation of crypto assets. INXY Payments has raised $4 million in funding, with Flashpoint Venture Capital as the investor. INXY Payments is an EU-licensed fintech platform providing multi-currency payment processing solutions for online businesses. Its services include funds gateways, large-scale payments, and currency exchange, supporting one-off and recurring transfers, automated payroll, and asset conversion. The platform emphasizes flexible integration via API, low-cost transactions, and compliance with mainstream auditing standards. It also provides reporting features suitable for accounting use, including complete transaction records and customized reports.
